docs.text:
Fluconazole, Itraconazole, Voriconazole, Posaconazole

Mechanism: Inhibit 14α-demethylase (fungal CYP450) → ↓ Ergosterol synthesis

Uses: - Fluconazole: Candida (except C. krusei), cryptococcal meningitis (AIDS), prophylaxis - Itraconazole: Aspergillus, Blastomyces, Histoplasma, Sporothrix, onychomycosis - Voriconazole: DOC for Aspergillus, Candida (including fluconazole-resistant)

Adverse Effects: - Hepatotoxicity - Drug interactions: CYP450 inhibitors - Voriconazole: Visual disturbances, photosensitivity

NEET PG: - Voriconazole: DOC for Aspergillus - Fluconazole: Ineffective against Aspergillus, C. krusei

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. DOC for MRSA? Answer: Vancomycin

  2. Only cephalosporin covering MRSA? Answer: Ceftaroline (5th generation)

  3. DOC for Listeria? Answer: Ampicillin

  4. Antibiotic causing red man syndrome? Answer: Vancomycin

  5. DOC for Rickettsiae? Answer: Doxycycline

  6. Antibiotic safe in penicillin allergy? Answer: Aztreonam

  7. Most common adverse effect of aminoglycosides? Answer: Nephrotoxicity & ototoxicity

  8. Anti-TB drug causing optic neuritis? Answer: Ethambutol

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know mechanism of all major antibiotic classes
  • Memorize spectrum of coverage
  • Learn drug of choice for common infections
  • Understand adverse effects
  • Know TB treatment regimens
  • Learn resistance mechanisms
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on antimicrobials

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 5-7 questions)

docs.title: CARDIOVASCULAR DRUGS - NEET PG Study Material
Classification
docs.text:
1. Diuretics
  2. Sympathoplegics (β-blockers, α-blockers, Central acting)
  3. Vasodilators (ACE-I, ARBs, CCBs, Direct vasodilators)
  4. Others

docs.title: Thiazide Diuretics
docs.text:
Prototype: Hydrochlorothiazide, Chlorthalidone, Indapamide

Site of action: Distal convoluted tubule (DCT) Mechanism: Block Na-Cl cotransporter (NCC)

Effects: - Moderate diuresis - ↓ BP (first-line for hypertension) - ↑ Ca2+ reabsorption (prevent kidney stones)

Uses: - Hypertension (first-line, especially elderly) - Edema (CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome) - Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (paradoxical effect) - Hypercalciuria (recurrent kidney stones)

Adverse effects: - Hypokalemia (most common) - Hyponatremia - Hypercalcemia - Hyperglycemia (worsen diabetes) - Hyperuricemia (precipitate gout) - Hyperlipidemia - Hypochloremic alkalosis - Erectile dysfunction

Contraindications: Gout, hypokalemia

NEET PG High-Yield: - First-line for hypertension (JNC 8) - Hypercalcemia (opposite of loop diuretics) - Used in nephrogenic DI (paradoxical) - 4 Hypers: Hyperglycemia, Hyperlipidemia, Hyperuricemia, Hypercalcemia

docs.title: Loop Diuretics
docs.text:
Drugs: Furosemide, Bumetanide, Torsemide, Ethacrynic acid

Site: Thick ascending limb of loop of Henle (TAL) Mechanism: Block Na-K-2Cl cotransporter (NKCC2)

Effects: - Most efficacious diuretics ("high ceiling") - ↑ Ca2+ excretion (hypocalcemia) - ↓ Renal concentrating ability

Uses: - Acute pulmonary edema (IV furosemide - DOC) - Acute renal failure (maintain urine output) - Hypercalcemia (↑ Ca2+ excretion) - CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome - Cerebral edema

Adverse effects: - Hypokalemia (arrhythmias) - Hypocalcemia - Hypomagnesemia - Metabolic alkalosis - Ototoxicity (especially with aminoglycosides) - Hyperuricemia (gout)

NEET PG High-Yield: - DOC for acute pulmonary edema - Most efficacious diuretics - Hypocalcemia (opposite of thiazides) - Ototoxicity (ethacrynic acid > furosemide) - Used in hypercalcemia

docs.title: A. Aldosterone Antagonists
docs.text:
Drugs: Spironolactone, Eplerenone

Site: Collecting duct Mechanism: Block aldosterone receptors

Effects: - Mild diuresis - ↓ K+ excretion (hyperkalemia risk) - Anti-androgenic (spironolactone)

Uses: - Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn's syndrome) - CHF (mortality benefit - RALES trial) - Cirrhosis with ascites (DOC) - Hirsutism, acne (spironolactone - anti-androgen)

Adverse effects: - Hyperkalemia (most serious) - Gynecomastia (spironolactone - anti-androgen) - Metabolic acidosis

NEET PG: - Spironolactone: Mortality benefit in CHF - DOC for ascites in cirrhosis - Gynecomastia (spironolactone, NOT eplerenone)

docs.title: B. ENaC Blockers
docs.text:
Drugs: Amiloride, Triamterene

Mechanism: Block epithelial Na+ channels (ENaC)

Uses: - With thiazides (prevent hypokalemia) - Lithium-induced nephrogenic DI

NEET PG: Amiloride blocks ENaC (direct effect, not via aldosterone)

docs.title: Osmotic Diuretics
docs.text:
Drug: Mannitol

Mechanism: Osmotic effect (not reabsorbed)

Uses: - Cerebral edema (↓ ICP) - Acute glaucoma (↓ intraocular pressure) - Prevent acute renal failure (during surgery, rhabdomyolysis)

Adverse effects: - Pulmonary edema (initial ↑ plasma volume) - Hyponatremia (dilutional)

Contraindications: Anuria, pulmonary edema, CHF

NEET PG: - DOC for cerebral edema & acute glaucoma - Contraindicated in anuria (will worsen)

docs.title: Beta-Blockers
docs.text:
Classification: 1. Non-selective (β1 + β2): Propranolol, nadolol, timolol 2. Selective β1: Atenolol, Metoprolol, bisoprolol, esmolol 3. α + β blockers: Labetalol, carvedilol

Mnemonic for selective β1: "**A**ccepted **M**etoprolol **B**isoprolol **E**smolol"

Mechanism: Block β-adrenergic receptors - ↓ Heart rate (β1) - ↓ Contractility (β1) - ↓ Renin release (β1) - ↓ AV conduction (β1)

Uses: - Hypertension (especially with CAD, post-MI) - Angina (↓ myocardial O2 demand) - Post-MI (↓ mortality) - CHF (bisoprolol, metoprolol, carvedilol - mortality benefit) - Arrhythmias (SVT, AF, ventricular arrhythmias) - Hyperthyroidism (symptomatic relief) - Migraine prophylaxis (propranolol) - Essential tremor (propranolol) - Glaucoma (timolol eye drops) - Variceal bleeding (propranolol - ↓ portal pressure)

Adverse effects: - Bronchospasm (β2 blockade - avoid in asthma) - Bradycardia, AV block - Mask hypoglycemia (in diabetics) - Worsen peripheral vascular disease - Fatigue, depression - Erectile dysfunction - Rebound hypertension (abrupt withdrawal)

Contraindications: - Asthma, COPD (non-selective) - Heart block (2nd/3rd degree) - Cardiogenic shock - Severe bradycardia

NEET PG High-Yield: - Mortality benefit: Post-MI & CHF - Contraindicated in asthma (bronchospasm) - Selective β1: Atenolol, Metoprolol (safer in COPD) - Labetalol: α + β blocker (DOC in pregnancy) - Esmolol: Ultra-short acting (IV, for arrhythmias)

docs.title: ACE Inhibitors
docs.text:
Drugs: Enalapril, Lisinopril, Ramipril, Captopril, Perindopril

Mechanism: - Inhibit ACE (Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme) - Block: Angiotensin I → Angiotensin II - ↓ Degradation of bradykinin (accumulates)

Effects: - ↓ BP (vasodilation) - ↓ Aldosterone (↓ Na+ retention, ↑ K+) - ↓ Cardiac remodeling - Renoprotective (↓ intraglomerular pressure)

Uses: - Hypertension (first-line, especially with diabetes/CKD) - CHF (mortality benefit - reduce afterload) - Post-MI (prevent remodeling) - Diabetic nephropathy (slow progression) - Proteinuria (↓ intraglomerular pressure)

Adverse effects: - Dry cough (10-20%) - Due to ↑ bradykinin (most common side effect) - Hyperkalemia (↓ aldosterone) - Angioedema (rare but serious - due to ↑ bradykinin) - Acute renal failure in bilateral renal artery stenosis - Teratogenic (fetal renal dysgenesis - contraindicated in pregnancy) - First-dose hypotension - Taste alteration (captopril)

Contraindications: - Pregnancy (Category D) - Bilateral renal artery stenosis - Hyperkalemia - Angioedema (history)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Dry cough: Most common side effect (switch to ARB) - Teratogenic (avoid in pregnancy) - Renoprotective (slow diabetic nephropathy) - Contraindicated in bilateral RAS (GFR depends on efferent constriction) - Mortality benefit: CHF & post-MI

docs.title: Angiotensin Receptor Blockers (ARBs)
docs.text:
Drugs: Losartan, Telmisartan, Valsartan, Irbesartan, Candesartan

Mechanism: Block AT1 receptors (Angiotensin II receptors)

Advantages over ACE-I: - No cough (do not affect bradykinin) - No angioedema (rare) - Better tolerated

Uses: Same as ACE inhibitors - Hypertension (alternative to ACE-I) - CHF - Diabetic nephropathy - Post-MI

Adverse effects: Similar to ACE-I (but no cough/angioedema) - Hyperkalemia - Teratogenic (contraindicated in pregnancy) - Hypotension

NEET PG: - Alternative to ACE-I (if cough develops) - No cough or angioedema (doesn't affect bradykinin) - Losartan: Also ↓ uric acid (good for gout with HTN)

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
1. Dihydropyridines (vascular-selective): - Amlodipine, Nifedipine, Felodipine, Nicardipine, Nimodipine

2. Non-dihydropyridines (cardiac-selective): - Verapamil, Diltiazem

Mechanism: Block L-type Ca2+ channels - Dihydropyridines: Peripheral vasodilation (↓ afterload) - Verapamil/Diltiazem: ↓ Heart rate, ↓ contractility, ↓ AV conduction

docs.title: Dihydropyridines (Amlodipine, Nifedipine)
docs.text:
Uses: - Hypertension (especially elderly, isolated systolic HTN) - Variant (Prinzmetal's) angina (coronary vasospasm) - Raynaud's phenomenon

Adverse effects: - Reflex tachycardia (nifedipine > amlodipine) - Peripheral edema (ankle swelling) - Flushing, headache - Gingival hyperplasia

NEET PG: - DOC for variant angina (Prinzmetal's) - Reflex tachycardia (peripheral vasodilation) - Safe in asthma (unlike β-blockers)

docs.title: Non-Dihydropyridines (Verapamil, Diltiazem)
docs.text:
Uses: - Hypertension - Angina (stable, unstable) - Supraventricular arrhythmias (SVT, AF rate control) - Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (verapamil)

Adverse effects: - Constipation (verapamil - most common) - Bradycardia, AV block - Worsening of heart failure (negative inotrope)

Contraindications: - Heart block (2nd/3rd degree) - Do NOT combine with β-blockers (↑ risk of bradycardia, heart block)

NEET PG: - Verapamil: Constipation (most common side effect) - DOC for SVT (verapamil/diltiazem) - Contraindicated with β-blockers (additive cardiac depression)

docs.title: Nitrates
docs.text:
Drugs: Nitroglycerin (sublingual, IV, transdermal), Isosorbide dinitrate, Isosorbide mononitrate

Mechanism: - Release nitric oxide (NO) - ↑ cGMP → Smooth muscle relaxation - Venodilation > Arteriodilation

Effects: - ↓ Preload (venodilation) - ↓ myocardial O2 demand - ↓ Afterload (mild arteriodilation) - Coronary vasodilation

Uses: - Acute angina (sublingual nitroglycerin - DOC) - Prophylaxis of angina (long-acting nitrates) - Acute MI (IV nitroglycerin) - Acute pulmonary edema (↓ preload) - Esophageal spasm

Adverse effects: - Headache (most common - cerebral vasodilation) - Postural hypotension (reflex tachycardia) - Tolerance (with continuous use - give nitrate-free interval) - Methemoglobinemia (high doses)

Contraindications: - Sildenafil (Viagra) - Severe hypotension (both ↑ cGMP) - Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy - Severe aortic stenosis - Right ventricular infarction (preload-dependent)

NEET PG High-Yield: - DOC for acute angina (sublingual nitroglycerin) - Headache: Most common side effect - Tolerance: Give nitrate-free interval (10-12 hrs) - Contraindicated with sildenafil (PDE-5 inhibitor)

docs.title: Drugs that ↓ Mortality in CHF
docs.text:
1. ACE inhibitors (or ARBs)
  2. β-blockers (bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol)
  3. Aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone)
  4. ARNI (Angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor - sacubitril/valsartan)
  5. SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin)

NEET PG: These 5 drug classes ↓ mortality in CHF

docs.title: Cardiac Glycosides (Digoxin)
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Inhibits Na+/K+-ATPase pump - ↑ Intracellular Na+ → ↑ Ca2+ (via Na+/Ca2+ exchanger) - Positive inotrope (↑ contractility) - Negative chronotrope (↓ heart rate via ↑ vagal tone) - ↓ AV conduction

Uses: - CHF with AF (rate control + inotropy) - Systolic HF (improve symptoms, no mortality benefit)

Adverse effects: - Arrhythmias (most serious): VPCs, VT, AV block - GI: Nausea, vomiting, anorexia - CNS: Confusion, visual disturbances (yellow-green vision - xanthopsia) - Gynecomastia

Toxicity: - Predisposing factors: Hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, hypomagnesemia, renal failure - ECG: Scooping of ST segment, T wave inversion - Treatment: Stop digoxin, Digoxin-specific Fab antibodies, correct electrolytes

Contraindications: WPW syndrome, AV block

NEET PG High-Yield: - No mortality benefit in CHF (improves symptoms only) - Yellow-green vision (xanthopsia) - classic toxicity - Hypokalemia worsens toxicity (competes for Na+/K+-ATPase) - Treatment of toxicity: Digoxin-specific Fab antibodies

docs.title: IA (Moderate blockade, ↑ APD)
docs.text:
Drugs: Quinidine, Procainamide, Disopyramide

Uses: Atrial & ventricular arrhythmias Adverse effects: - Quinidine: Cinchonism, torsades de pointes, diarrhea - Procainamide: Drug-induced lupus, agranulocytosis

docs.title: IB (Weak blockade, ↓ APD)
docs.text:
Drugs: Lidocaine, Mexiletine

Uses: Ventricular arrhythmias (post-MI) Route: IV only (high first-pass metabolism) Adverse effects: CNS (seizures), cardiac depression

NEET PG: Lidocaine for ventricular arrhythmias (IV)

docs.title: IC (Strong blockade, no change in APD)
docs.text:
Drugs: Flecainide, Propafenone

Uses: Supraventricular arrhythmias, AF Contraindications: Structural heart disease, post-MI (↑ mortality)

docs.title: Class II: Beta-Blockers
docs.text:
Drugs: Propranolol, atenolol, metoprolol, esmolol

Uses: SVT, AF (rate control), ventricular arrhythmias

NEET PG: Esmolol - ultra-short acting (t½ = 9 min)

docs.title: Class III: Potassium Channel Blockers (↑ APD)
docs.text:
Drugs: Amiodarone, Sotalol, Dofetilide, Ibutilide

Amiodarone: - Most effective antiarrhythmic - Uses: All arrhythmias (atrial & ventricular), refractory VF/VT - Adverse effects: - Pulmonary fibrosis (most serious) - Thyroid dysfunction (hypo/hyperthyroidism - contains iodine) - Hepatotoxicity - Corneal deposits (reversible) - Photosensitivity (blue-gray skin) - Bradycardia

NEET PG High-Yield: - Most effective antiarrhythmic - Pulmonary fibrosis - most serious side effect - Contains iodine (thyroid dysfunction)

docs.title: Class IV: Calcium Channel Blockers
docs.text:
Drugs: Verapamil, Diltiazem

Uses: SVT, AF (rate control)

docs.title: Others (Not in Vaughan Williams)
docs.text:
Adenosine: - DOC for acute SVT (AVNRT, AVRT) - Ultra-short t½ (< 10 seconds) - Mechanism: Activates K+ channels, ↑ AV block - Adverse effects: Flushing, dyspnea, chest pain (transient) - Contraindicated: Asthma (bronchospasm), WPW with AF

NEET PG: Adenosine - DOC for acute SVT (ultra-short acting)

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. First-line antihypertensive in elderly? Answer: Thiazide diuretic

  2. DOC for acute pulmonary edema? Answer: IV Furosemide

  3. Most common side effect of ACE inhibitors? Answer: Dry cough

  4. Antihypertensive contraindicated in pregnancy? Answer: ACE inhibitors, ARBs

  5. DOC for acute angina? Answer: Sublingual nitroglycerin

  6. Drugs with mortality benefit in CHF? Answer: ACE-I, β-blockers, Spironolactone, ARNI, SGLT2-I

  7. Most effective antiarrhythmic? Answer: Amiodarone

  8. DOC for acute SVT? Answer: Adenosine

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know diuretic sites of action
  • Understand ACE-I vs ARB differences
  • Learn β-blocker contraindications
  • Know CCB classification
  • Memorize drugs with CHF mortality benefit
  • Understand digoxin toxicity
  • Learn Vaughan Williams classification
  • Know antiarrhythmic drug uses
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on CVS drugs

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (6-8 questions)

docs.title: RENAL PHYSIOLOGY - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: Kidneys regulate: - Fluid & electrolyte balance - Acid-base balance - Blood pressure - RBC production (EPO) - Vitamin D activation - Excretion of waste products

docs.title: Nephron Structure
docs.text:
Glomerulus → PCT → Loop of Henle → DCT → Collecting Duct
               ↓         ↓           ↓          ↓
            65-70%     15-20%      5-10%      5-10%
          (Na+ reabs) (Concen)  (Fine-tune) (ADH/Aldo)

Types of Nephrons: 1. Cortical (85%): Short loop of Henle 2. Juxtamedullary (15%): Long loop, important for concentration

docs.title: Renal Blood Flow (RBF)
docs.text:
Normal RBF: 1200 ml/min (20-25% of cardiac output) Renal Plasma Flow (RPF): 600-700 ml/min

Formula: RBF = CO × 0.20-0.25

Autoregulation: Maintains constant RBF despite BP changes (80-180 mmHg)

Mechanisms: 1. Myogenic: Stretch of afferent arteriole → vasoconstriction 2. Tubuloglomerular feedback: ↑NaCl at macula densa → ↓GFR

NEET PG: Kidney has best autoregulation among all organs

docs.title: Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
docs.text: Normal GFR: 125 ml/min or 180 L/day Filtration Fraction (FF): GFR/RPF = 125/600 = 20%

docs.title: Starling Forces
docs.text:
Favoring Filtration: - Glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure (PGC) = 60 mmHg (main driving force)

Opposing Filtration: - Bowman's capsule hydrostatic pressure (PBS) = 18 mmHg - Glomerular capillary oncotic pressure (πGC) = 32 mmHg

Net Filtration Pressure = PGC - (PBS + πGC) = 60 - (18 + 32) = 10 mmHg

GFR = Kf × Net Filtration Pressure

docs.title: Factors Affecting GFR
docs.text:
Factor Effect on GFR Mechanism ↑ Afferent arteriole dilation ↑ ↑ PGC ↑ Efferent arteriole constriction ↑ ↑ PGC ↓ Plasma proteins ↑ ↓ πGC Urinary obstruction ↓ ↑ PBS ↓ Renal blood flow ↓ ↓ PGC ACE inhibitors ↓ Dilate efferent (↓ PGC) NSAIDs ↓ Constrict afferent (↓ PGC)

NEET PG: - Angiotensin II: Constricts efferent > afferent → Maintains GFR - ACE inhibitors: Dilate efferent → ↓ GFR (contraindicated in bilateral RAS)

docs.title: Inulin Clearance (Gold Standard)
docs.text:
• Freely filtered
  • Not reabsorbed or secreted
  • GFR = Urine [Inulin] × Urine flow / Plasma [Inulin]

docs.title: Creatinine Clearance (Clinical)
docs.text:
• Slightly overestimates GFR (small amount secreted by PCT)
  • Normal: 90-140 ml/min

Cockcroft-Gault Formula: 

CrCl = [(140 - Age) × Weight in kg] / (72 × Serum Cr)
       × 0.85 (for females)

NEET PG: Creatinine clearance overestimates true GFR (secreted in PCT)

docs.title: PROXIMAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE (PCT)
docs.text:
Reabsorbs 65-70% of filtered load

Substances Reabsorbed: - Na+ (65-70%): Na+/K+ ATPase (basolateral) - Water (65-70%): Follows Na+ (osmosis via aquaporin-1) - Glucose (100%): SGLT2 (sodium-glucose cotransporter 2) - Amino acids (100%) - Bicarbonate (80-90%): Via carbonic anhydrase - Phosphate, uric acid, K+

Substances Secreted: - Organic acids: Uric acid, drugs (penicillin, probenecid) - Organic bases: Creatinine - H+ ions

NEET PG High-Yield: - Glucose Tm (Transport maximum) = 375 mg/min - Renal threshold for glucose = 180 mg/dl - SGLT2 inhibitors (gliflozins): Block glucose reabsorption → glycosuria

docs.title: Descending Limb
docs.text:
• Permeable to water (aquaporin-1)
  • Impermeable to NaCl
  • Water reabsorbed → Urine becomes concentrated

docs.title: Thin Ascending Limb
docs.text:
• Permeable to NaCl
  • Impermeable to water

docs.title: Thick Ascending Limb (TAL)
docs.text:
• Impermeable to water ("diluting segment")
  • Active Na+ reabsorption via Na-K-2Cl cotransporter (NKCC2)
  • Reabsorbs 15-20% of filtered Na+
  • Creates medullary hypertonicity (countercurrent multiplier)

NEET PG: - Loop diuretics (furosemide): Block NKCC2 in TAL - TAL impermeable to water → "Diluting segment"

docs.title: DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE (DCT)
docs.text:
Reabsorbs 5-10% of filtered Na+

Mechanisms: - Na-Cl cotransporter (NCC) - Impermeable to water - Ca2+ reabsorption (stimulated by PTH)

NEET PG: - Thiazide diuretics: Block NCC in DCT - Site of Ca2+ reabsorption (PTH-dependent) - Gitelman syndrome: Defect in NCC

docs.title: COLLECTING DUCT
docs.text: Site of fine-tuning (reabsorbs 5-10% Na+)

docs.title: Principal Cells
docs.text:
Function: Na+ reabsorption, K+ secretion, water reabsorption

Transporters: - ENaC (Epithelial Na+ Channel): Na+ reabsorption - ROMK: K+ secretion - Aquaporin-2 (AQP2): Water reabsorption (ADH-dependent)

NEET PG: - Aldosterone: ↑ ENaC → ↑ Na+ reabsorption, ↑ K+ secretion - ADH (Vasopressin): Inserts AQP2 → ↑ water reabsorption - Amiloride: Blocks ENaC (K+ sparing diuretic)

docs.title: Intercalated Cells
docs.text:
Function: Acid-base balance

Type A (in acidosis): - Secrete H+ (via H+-ATPase) - Reabsorb HCO3-

Type B (in alkalosis): - Secrete HCO3- - Reabsorb H+

docs.title: Countercurrent Multiplier (Loop of Henle)
docs.text:
Function: Creates medullary hypertonicity (300 → 1200 mOsm/kg)

Mechanism: 1. TAL actively pumps out NaCl (NKCC2) 2. Water cannot follow (impermeable) 3. Interstitium becomes hypertonic 4. Descending limb loses water to interstitium 5. Urine becomes concentrated

NEET PG: Loop diuretics abolish countercurrent multiplier

docs.title: Countercurrent Exchanger (Vasa Recta)
docs.text: Function: Maintains medullary hypertonicity - Minimizes washout of solutes from medullary interstitium

docs.title: Maximum Urine Concentration
docs.text: With ADH: 1200 mOsm/kg (4× plasma) - Collecting duct permeable to water (AQP2 inserted) - Water reabsorbed into hypertonic medulla - Small volume, concentrated urine

docs.title: Maximum Urine Dilution
docs.text:
Without ADH: 50 mOsm/kg - Collecting duct impermeable to water - Water not reabsorbed - Large volume, dilute urine

NEET PG: Urine can be 24× diluted (50 mOsm) but only 4× concentrated (1200 mOsm)

docs.title: 1. ADH (Vasopressin)
docs.text:
Source: Posterior pituitary Stimulus: ↑ Plasma osmolality (>280 mOsm), ↓ Blood volume

Mechanism: - Inserts aquaporin-2 in collecting duct - ↑ Water reabsorption - Concentrated urine

NEET PG: - Central DI: No ADH production - Nephrogenic DI: Kidneys don't respond to ADH - SIADH: Excess ADH → Hyponatremia

docs.title: 2. Aldosterone
docs.text:
Source: Adrenal cortex (zona glomerulosa) Stimulus: ↑ Angiotensin II, ↑ K+, ↓ Na+

Mechanism: - ↑ ENaC in collecting duct - ↑ Na+ reabsorption, ↑ K+ secretion - ↑ Blood pressure

NEET PG: - Primary hyperaldosteronism: Hypertension, hypokalemia - Conn's syndrome: Aldosterone-secreting adenoma

docs.title: 3. PTH (Parathyroid Hormone)
docs.text: Effects on Kidney: - ↑ Ca2+ reabsorption (DCT) - ↓ Phosphate reabsorption (PCT) - ↑ 1α-hydroxylase → Active vitamin D

docs.title: 4. ANP (Atrial Natriuretic Peptide)
docs.text:
Source: Atrial myocytes Stimulus: ↑ Atrial stretch (volume overload)

Effects: - ↑ GFR (dilates afferent, constricts efferent) - ↓ Na+ reabsorption - ↓ Renin, ↓ Aldosterone - ↓ ADH - Net: Natriuresis & diuresis

docs.title: Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System (RAAS)
docs.text:
↓ BP, ↓ Na+, ↑ Sympathetic
         ↓
   Juxtaglomerular cells
         ↓
      RENIN
         ↓
Angiotensinogen → Angiotensin I
                      ↓ (ACE in lungs)
                 Angiotensin II
         ┌────────┴────────┐
    Vasoconstriction   Aldosterone
    (↑ BP)             (↑ Na+, ↑ BP)

Effects of Angiotensin II: 1. Systemic vasoconstriction 2. Efferent > afferent arteriole constriction (maintains GFR) 3. ↑ Aldosterone secretion 4. ↑ ADH secretion 5. ↑ Proximal tubule Na+ reabsorption 6. ↑ Thirst

NEET PG: - ACE inhibitors: Block formation of Angiotensin II - ARBs: Block Angiotensin II receptors - Both cause: ↓ BP, ↑ K+ (less aldosterone), ↓ GFR

docs.title: Reabsorption
docs.text:
• PCT: 65% (paracellular)
  • TAL: 25% (via NKCC2)
  • DCT: 5%

docs.title: Secretion
docs.text:
• Collecting duct: Principal cells (via ROMK)
  • Regulated by aldosterone

Factors ↑ K+ Secretion: - ↑ Aldosterone - ↑ Tubular flow rate - ↑ Plasma K+ - Metabolic alkalosis

NEET PG: - Loop diuretics: ↑ K+ loss (↑ flow to collecting duct) - Aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone): Hyperkalemia

docs.title: Calcium
docs.text:
Reabsorption: - PCT: 60-70% (paracellular) - TAL: 20% (paracellular) - DCT: 10% (PTH-stimulated, active transport)

NEET PG: - Thiazides: ↑ Ca2+ reabsorption → Hypocalciuria - Loop diuretics: ↓ Ca2+ reabsorption → Hypercalciuria

docs.title: Phosphate
docs.text:
Reabsorption: - PCT: 80% (via Na-Pi cotransporter) - Inhibited by PTH & FGF-23

NEET PG: PTH causes phosphaturia (↓ phosphate reabsorption)

docs.title: H+ Secretion
docs.text:
• PCT: 80-90% HCO3- reabsorption (via carbonic anhydrase)
  • Collecting duct: H+ secretion (type A intercalated cells)

docs.title: HCO3- Reabsorption
docs.text:
Tubular lumen    |    Cell    |    Blood
                 |            |
H+ + HCO3- → H2CO3 → CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 → H+ + HCO3-
                 |  (CA)      |   (CA)    |
                 |            |           → HCO3- reabsorbed

NEET PG: Acetazolamide inhibits carbonic anhydrase → ↓ HCO3- reabsorption → Alkaline urine

docs.title: Clearance Concepts - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Clearance: Volume of plasma completely cleared of a substance per unit time

Formula: C = (U × V) / P - U = Urine concentration - V = Urine flow rate - P = Plasma concentration

docs.title: Renal Handling
docs.text:
Substance Clearance vs GFR Handling Inulin C = GFR Filtered only Glucose C = 0 Filtered, 100% reabsorbed PAH C > GFR Filtered + secreted Urea C < GFR Filtered, partially reabsorbed Creatinine C ≈ GFR (slight >) Filtered, small secretion

NEET PG: - PAH clearance = Renal plasma flow (RPF) when fully secreted - Inulin clearance = GFR (gold standard)

docs.title: Diuretics - Quick Reference
docs.text: Diuretic Site Mechanism Effects Acetazolamide PCT ↓ Carbonic anhydrase Alkaline urine, ↓ HCO3- Loop (Furosemide) TAL Block NKCC2 Hypokalemia, hypocalcemia, metabolic alkalosis Thiazides DCT Block NCC Hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, metabolic alkalosis K-sparing (Amiloride) CD Block ENaC Hyperkalemia Spironolactone CD Block aldosterone Hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Normal GFR? Answer: 125 ml/min

  2. Filtration fraction? Answer: 20% (GFR/RPF)

  3. Maximum glucose reabsorption occurs at? Answer: PCT (via SGLT2)

  4. Diluting segment of nephron? Answer: Thick ascending limb

  5. ADH acts on which aquaporin? Answer: Aquaporin-2 (collecting duct)

  6. Loop diuretics block which transporter? Answer: NKCC2 (Na-K-2Cl)

  7. Site of PTH action for Ca2+ reabsorption? Answer: DCT

  8. Gold standard for GFR measurement? Answer: Inulin clearance

docs.title: PCT Reabsorption
docs.text: "PCT Gets All the Good Stuff" - 65-70% of Na+, water - 100% glucose, amino acids - 80-90% HCO3-

docs.title: Diuretics Order (Proximal to Distal)
docs.text: "A**ce **L**oopy **T**hieves **S**pared **K**idneys" - **A**cetazolamide (PCT) - **L**oop diuretics (TAL) - **T**hiazides (DCT) - **S**pironolactone (CD) - **K-sparing (CD)

docs.title: ADH Effects
docs.text: "ADH Adds H2O" - Inserts AQP2 → Water reabsorption

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Understand GFR and Starling forces
  • Memorize normal values (GFR, RBF, RPF, FF)
  • Know functions of each nephron segment
  • Learn diuretic sites of action
  • Understand RAAS pathway
  • Know ADH and aldosterone mechanisms
  • Learn clearance concepts
  • Understand countercurrent mechanism
  • Practice calculations (CrCl, clearance)
  • Solve 40+ MCQs on renal physiology

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 4-6 questions)

docs.title: RESPIRATORY PHYSIOLOGY - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: Functions of Respiratory System: 1. Gas exchange (O2 and CO2) 2. Acid-base balance (CO2 excretion) 3. Phonation (voice production) 4. Olfaction (smell) 5. Heat & water loss regulation 6. Metabolism (ACE in lungs)

docs.title: Volumes (cannot be subdivided)
docs.text:
Volume Definition Value Measured by Tidal Volume (TV) Volume inspired/expired in normal breathing 500 ml Spirometry Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) Extra volume that can be inspired 3000 ml Spirometry Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV) Extra volume that can be expired 1100 ml Spirometry Residual Volume (RV) Volume remaining after maximal expiration 1200 ml Body plethysmography

NEET PG: RV cannot be measured by spirometry (requires body plethysmography or helium dilution)

docs.title: Capacities (sum of 2 or more volumes)
docs.text:
Capacity Formula Value Significance Inspiratory Capacity (IC) TV + IRV 3500 ml Maximum inspiration from FRC Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) ERV + RV 2300 ml Lung volume at end of normal expiration Vital Capacity (VC) TV + IRV + ERV 4600 ml Maximum breath (inspiration → expiration) Total Lung Capacity (TLC) VC + RV (or IC + FRC) 5800 ml Total lung volume

NEET PG High-Yield: - FRC = ERV + RV: Most important capacity (gas exchange occurs here) - VC: Decreased in restrictive lung disease - RV/TLC ratio: Increased in obstructive lung disease

docs.title: Spirometry Values
docs.text:
Forced Vital Capacity (FVC): Maximum volume expired forcefully after max inspiration FEV1 (Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 sec): Volume expired in first second of FVC FEV1/FVC ratio: Normally >70% (0.7)

NEET PG: - Obstructive disease: ↓ FEV1/FVC (<70%) - Restrictive disease: Normal or ↑ FEV1/FVC, ↓ FVC

docs.title: Anatomical Dead Space
docs.text: Definition: Air in conducting airways (no gas exchange) Volume: 150 ml (≈ 2 ml/kg) Includes: Nose, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles (up to terminal bronchioles)

docs.title: Alveolar Dead Space
docs.text: Definition: Ventilated alveoli with no/poor perfusion Normal: Negligible (≈ 0) Increased in: Pulmonary embolism, shock

docs.title: Physiological Dead Space
docs.text:
Definition: Anatomical dead space + Alveolar dead space Formula: VD/VT = (PaCO2 - PECO2) / PaCO2 (Bohr equation)

NEET PG: - Anatomical dead space: 150 ml (⅓ of tidal volume) - Alveolar ventilation = (TV - Dead space) × RR = (500-150) × 12 = 4200 ml/min

docs.title: Minute Ventilation
docs.text: Definition: Total volume breathed per minute Formula: VE = TV × RR = 500 × 12 = 6000 ml/min (6 L/min)

docs.title: Alveolar Ventilation
docs.text:
Definition: Volume reaching alveoli per minute (effective ventilation) Formula: VA = (TV - VD) × RR = (500-150) × 12 = 4200 ml/min

NEET PG: Alveolar ventilation more important than minute ventilation

docs.title: Inspiratory Muscles
docs.text:
Primary: - Diaphragm (most important - 75% of inspiration) - Nerve: Phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5) - Contraction → Descends → ↑ Vertical diameter - External intercostals - Elevate ribs → ↑ AP & transverse diameter

Accessory (in dyspnea): - Sternocleidomastoid - Scalenes - Pectoralis major/minor

NEET PG: "C3, 4, 5 keep the diaphragm alive" (phrenic nerve)

docs.title: Expiratory Muscles
docs.text:
Normal breathing: Passive (elastic recoil)

Active expiration: - Internal intercostals: Depress ribs - Abdominal muscles: Compress abdomen, push diaphragm up

docs.title: Compliance (C)
docs.text:
Definition: Change in volume per unit change in pressure Formula: C = ΔV / ΔP Normal: 200 ml/cm H2O

Increased compliance (floppy lungs): - Emphysema (loss of elastic tissue) - Easy to inflate, difficult to deflate

Decreased compliance (stiff lungs): - Pulmonary fibrosis - Pulmonary edema - ARDS - Difficult to inflate

NEET PG: Emphysema = ↑ Compliance, Fibrosis = ↓ Compliance

docs.title: Elastance
docs.text: Definition: Reciprocal of compliance (E = 1/C) Increased in: Pulmonary fibrosis (stiff lungs)

docs.title: SURFACTANT - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Produced by: Type II pneumocytes Composition: 90% lipid (dipalmitoyl phosphatidylcholine - DPPC), 10% protein

Functions: 1. ↓ Surface tension in alveoli 2. Prevents alveolar collapse (especially small alveoli) 3. Increases compliance 4. Prevents pulmonary edema (opposes transudation)

Laplace's Law: P = 2T/r - P = Pressure inside alveolus - T = Surface tension - r = Radius

Without surfactant: Smaller alveoli collapse (higher pressure needed) With surfactant: Surface tension ↓ more in small alveoli → Equalizes pressure

NEET PG High-Yield: - Surfactant deficiency: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (NRDS) - Treatment: Exogenous surfactant (beractant, poractant) - L/S ratio <2: Immature lungs (risk of NRDS) - Corticosteroids (betamethasone): Accelerate surfactant production

docs.title: VENTILATION-PERFUSION (V/Q) RATIO
docs.text:
Definition: Ratio of alveolar ventilation to pulmonary blood flow

Normal V/Q ratio: 0.8 (V = 4 L/min, Q = 5 L/min)

docs.title: Regional Variation
docs.text:
Apex: - High V/Q ratio (≈3) - Good ventilation, poor perfusion - High PO2, low PCO2 - Site for TB (Mycobacterium likes high O2)

Base: - Low V/Q ratio (≈0.6) - Good perfusion, better than ventilation - Low PO2, high PCO2

NEET PG: V/Q mismatch most common cause of hypoxemia

docs.title: Extreme V/Q States
docs.text:
Condition V/Q Ratio Cause Example Dead space ∞ (V, no Q) No perfusion Pulmonary embolism Shunt 0 (Q, no V) No ventilation Atelectasis, ARDS

Shunt: - Anatomical: Bronchial veins, Thebesian veins (2% of CO) - Physiological: Unventilated but perfused alveoli

NEET PG: - 100% O2 test: Hypoxemia due to shunt does NOT correct with 100% O2 - V/Q mismatch: Hypoxemia corrects with 100% O2

docs.title: Oxygen Transport
docs.text:
Forms: 1. Dissolved in plasma: 0.3 ml/100 ml blood (1.5%) 2. Bound to hemoglobin: 19.7 ml/100 ml blood (98.5%)

Total O2 content: 20 ml/100 ml (20 vol%)

Oxygen carrying capacity = Hb (g/dl) × 1.34 ml O2/g Hb O2 content = (Hb × 1.34 × SaO2) + (0.003 × PaO2)

NEET PG: Each gram of Hb binds 1.34 ml O2 when fully saturated

docs.title: Oxygen-Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Shape: Sigmoid (S-shaped) P50: PO2 at which Hb is 50% saturated = 27 mmHg

Plateau portion (PO2 >60 mmHg): - Large changes in PO2 → Small changes in SaO2 - Protects against hypoxemia (high altitude)

Steep portion (PO2 <60 mmHg): - Small changes in PO2 → Large O2 release to tissues

docs.title: Right Shift (↓ O2 affinity, ↑ O2 delivery to tissues)
docs.text:
Mnemonic: "**R**ight shift = **R**elease O2 to tissues"

Causes (CADET, face Right): - **C**O2 ↑ (hypercapnia) - **A**cid ↑ (↓ pH) - **D**PG (2,3-DPG) ↑ - **E**xercise - **T**emperature ↑

NEET PG: ↑ 2,3-DPG in chronic hypoxia, high altitude

docs.title: Left Shift (↑ O2 affinity, ↓ O2 delivery)
docs.text:
Causes: - ↓ Temperature (hypothermia) - ↓ CO2, ↓ H+ (alkalosis) - ↓ 2,3-DPG - Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) - Carboxyhemoglobin (CO poisoning) - Methemoglobin

NEET PG: - Fetal Hb: Left shift (higher O2 affinity - extracts O2 from maternal blood) - CO poisoning: Left shift + ↓ O2 capacity (binds 200× more than O2)

docs.title: Bohr Effect
docs.text:
Definition: ↑ CO2 & ↓ pH → ↓ O2 affinity (right shift) Mechanism: CO2 + H+ → Allosteric change in Hb → ↓ O2 binding Significance: Facilitates O2 unloading in tissues (↑ CO2, ↓ pH)

NEET PG: Bohr effect helps O2 delivery to metabolically active tissues

docs.title: Haldane Effect
docs.text:
Definition: Deoxygenated Hb binds more CO2 than oxygenated Hb Mechanism: Deoxygenated Hb is a better buffer for H+ Significance: Facilitates CO2 loading in tissues & unloading in lungs

NEET PG: Haldane effect explains 70% of CO2 transport difference between arterial & venous blood

docs.title: Carbon Dioxide Transport
docs.text:
Forms: 1. Dissolved in plasma: 7% (0.6 mmol/L per mmHg) 2. Carbamino compounds (bound to Hb): 23% 3. Bicarbonate (HCO3-): 70% (most important)

In RBCs: 

CO2 + H2O ⇌ H2CO3 ⇌ H+ + HCO3-
   (Carbonic anhydrase)

Chloride shift (Hamburger phenomenon): - HCO3- diffuses out of RBC → Cl- enters (maintain electroneutrality) - Occurs in tissues (CO2 loading) - Reversed in lungs

NEET PG: - 70% CO2 transported as HCO3- - Carbonic anhydrase: In RBCs, NOT in plasma - Chloride shift: Maintains electrical neutrality

docs.title: Medulla (Dorsal & Ventral Groups)
docs.text: Function: Basic rhythm of respiration - Dorsal respiratory group (DRG): Inspiration (stimulates phrenic nerve) - Ventral respiratory group (VRG): Expiration (active expiration)

docs.title: Pons
docs.text:
Pneumotaxic center: Limits inspiration (controls respiratory rate) Apneustic center: Prolongs inspiration

NEET PG: Medulla = Basic rhythm, Pons = Fine-tuning

docs.title: Central Chemoreceptors (Medulla)
docs.text:
Location: Ventral surface of medulla Stimulus: ↑ H+ in CSF (↑ CO2 crosses BBB → H2CO3 → H+) Response: ↑ Ventilation Contribution: 70-80% of hypercapnic drive

NEET PG: Central chemoreceptors respond to CO2 (via H+), NOT O2

docs.title: Peripheral Chemoreceptors
docs.text:
Location: - Carotid bodies (bifurcation of common carotid) - CN IX - Aortic bodies (arch of aorta) - CN X

Stimuli: 1. ↓ PO2 (most sensitive) - primary O2 sensor 2. ↑ PCO2 3. ↓ pH

Response: ↑ Ventilation Contribution: 30% of hypercapnic drive, 100% of hypoxic drive

NEET PG High-Yield: - Only peripheral chemoreceptors respond to hypoxemia - Carotid bodies: More sensitive to hypoxia than aortic bodies - Chronic hypercapnia (COPD): Lose central chemoreceptor sensitivity → Hypoxic drive predominates

docs.title: Other Receptors
docs.text:
Stretch receptors (Hering-Breuer reflex): - In airway smooth muscle - Inflation → Inhibit inspiration (prevents over-inflation)

Irritant receptors: - Rapid shallow breathing, cough, bronchoconstriction

J receptors (Juxtacapillary): - In alveolar walls - Stimulated by: Pulmonary edema, PE, pneumonia - Response: Rapid shallow breathing (tachypnea)

NEET PG: J receptors → Rapid shallow breathing in pulmonary edema

docs.title: HYPOXIA - TYPES
docs.text:
Type PaO2 CaO2 SaO2 Cause Example Hypoxic ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ Inspired O2, hypoventilation, V/Q mismatch, diffusion defect High altitude, COPD Anemic Normal ↓ Normal ↓ Hemoglobin Anemia Stagnant Normal Normal Normal ↓ Blood flow Shock, CHF Histotoxic Normal Normal Normal Cells can't use O2 Cyanide poisoning

NEET PG: - Hypoxic hypoxia: Only type with ↓ PaO2 - Anemic hypoxia: Normal PaO2 but ↓ O2 content - Cyanide poisoning: Inhibits cytochrome oxidase (can't use O2)

docs.title: Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS)
docs.text: Altitude: >2500m (8000 ft) Time: 6-12 hours after ascent Symptoms: Headache, nausea, dizziness, fatigue, insomnia

docs.title: High Altitude Pulmonary Edema (HAPE)
docs.text: Mechanism: Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction → ↑ Pulmonary artery pressure → Edema Features: Dyspnea, cough, frothy sputum Treatment: Descent, O2, nifedipine

docs.title: High Altitude Cerebral Edema (HACE)
docs.text:
Mechanism: Hypoxia → ↑ Cerebral blood flow → Edema Features: Ataxia, confusion, altered sensorium Treatment: Descent, O2, dexamethasone

NEET PG: Acetazolamide prevents AMS (causes metabolic acidosis → stimulates breathing)

docs.title: Acclimatization to High Altitude
docs.text:
Immediate: - ↑ Ventilation (hyperventilation) - ↑ Heart rate

Days-Weeks: - ↑ 2,3-DPG (right shift → ↑ O2 delivery) - ↑ Erythropoietin → Polycythemia - ↑ Capillary density

Months: - ↑ Mitochondria - ↑ Myoglobin

NEET PG: ↑ 2,3-DPG and polycythemia = Key adaptations

docs.title: Nitrogen Narcosis
docs.text: Cause: ↑ Nitrogen dissolves in tissues (high pressure) Depth: >30m (100 ft) Symptoms: Euphoria, impaired judgment ("rapture of the deep")

docs.title: Decompression Sickness (Bends)
docs.text:
Cause: Rapid ascent → Nitrogen bubbles form Features: - Type I: Joint pain, skin rash - Type II: CNS (paralysis), pulmonary (chokes) Treatment: Recompression (hyperbaric O2)

NEET PG: Decompression sickness prevented by slow ascent

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Anatomical dead space volume? Answer: 150 ml

  2. Alveolar ventilation formula? Answer: (TV - VD) × RR

  3. Normal V/Q ratio? Answer: 0.8

  4. What produces surfactant? Answer: Type II pneumocytes

  5. P50 of oxygen-hemoglobin curve? Answer: 27 mmHg

  6. Most common form of CO2 transport? Answer: Bicarbonate (70%)

  7. Primary stimulus for central chemoreceptors? Answer: ↑ H+ (from CO2)

  8. Only chemoreceptor responding to hypoxemia? Answer: Peripheral chemoreceptors

docs.title: Right Shift O2-Hb Curve
docs.text: "CADET, face **R**ight" - **C**O2 ↑ - **A**cid ↑ - **D**PG ↑ - **E**xercise - **T**emperature ↑

docs.title: Phrenic Nerve
docs.text: "C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive"

docs.title: CO2 Transport
docs.text: "70-23-7" - 70%: Bicarbonate - 23%: Carbamino - 7%: Dissolved

docs.title: V/Q Ratio
docs.text: "**A**pex = **A**ir (high V/Q), **B**ase = **B**lood (low V/Q)"

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Memorize lung volumes & capacities
  • Understand dead space concept
  • Know compliance changes in diseases
  • Learn surfactant functions
  • Understand V/Q ratio regional variation
  • Master O2-Hb dissociation curve
  • Know CO2 transport mechanisms
  • Learn chemoreceptor locations & stimuli
  • Understand types of hypoxia
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on respiratory physiology

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (5-7 questions)

docs.title: GASTROINTESTINAL PHYSIOLOGY - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: Functions of GI System: 1. Motility: Propulsion of food 2. Secretion: Enzymes, hormones, mucus 3. Digestion: Breakdown of nutrients 4. Absorption: Uptake of nutrients 5. Excretion: Elimination of waste

docs.title: Composition
docs.text:
Daily secretion: 1-1.5 L/day pH: 6-7 (slightly acidic to neutral)

Components: - Water: 99% - Electrolytes: Na+, K+, Cl-, HCO3- - Enzymes: - α-amylase (ptyalin): Starch → Maltose - Lingual lipase: Triglycerides - Mucins: Lubrication - Antibacterial: Lysozyme, lactoferrin, IgA

NEET PG: Salivary amylase (ptyalin) digests starch at pH 6-7, inactivated in stomach (pH <4)

docs.title: Salivary Glands
docs.text:
Gland % Contribution Type of Secretion Parotid 25% Serous (watery, enzyme-rich) Submandibular 70% Mixed (mainly serous) Sublingual 5% Mucous (viscous)

Nerve supply: - Parasympathetic (CN VII, IX): Stimulates copious watery secretion - Sympathetic: Small volume, viscous secretion

NEET PG: Parasympathetic = Main stimulator of salivation

docs.title: Functions of Saliva
docs.text:
1. Lubrication: Mucins
  2. Digestion: Amylase (starch)
  3. Protection: Lysozyme, IgA, buffer HCO3-
  4. Taste: Dissolves food
  5. Speech: Moistens mouth

docs.title: Gastric Juice Composition
docs.text:
Daily volume: 2-3 L pH: 1-2 (highly acidic)

Components: - HCl (hydrochloric acid) - Pepsinogen (inactive) → Pepsin (active) - Intrinsic factor (Vitamin B12 absorption) - Mucus (protection) - Bicarbonate (neutralization in mucus layer)

docs.title: Cell Types in Gastric Mucosa
docs.text:
Cell Type Location Secretion Function Parietal (Oxyntic) Body & Fundus HCl, Intrinsic factor Acid secretion, B12 absorption Chief (Peptic) Body & Fundus Pepsinogen, Gastric lipase Protein digestion Mucous neck cells Throughout Mucus, HCO3- Protection G cells Antrum Gastrin Stimulates HCl D cells Antrum & Body Somatostatin Inhibits gastrin & HCl ECL cells Body & Fundus Histamine Stimulates HCl

NEET PG High-Yield: - Parietal cells: HCl + Intrinsic factor - Chief cells: Pepsinogen - G cells: Gastrin (in antrum only)

docs.title: HCl Secretion Mechanism
docs.text:
Parietal Cell: 

H2O + CO2 → H2CO3 → H+ + HCO3-
         (Carbonic anhydrase)

H+ secreted into lumen via H+/K+-ATPase (Proton pump)
HCO3- absorbed into blood (alkaline tide)

Stimulators of HCl Secretion: 1. Gastrin (from G cells) - Most potent 2. Histamine (from ECL cells) - via H2 receptors 3. Acetylcholine (vagus nerve) - via M3 receptors

All act on parietal cells to stimulate H+/K+-ATPase (proton pump)

Inhibitors: 1. Somatostatin (from D cells) 2. Prostaglandins (PGE2) 3. Secretin (from duodenum)

NEET PG: - Proton pump (H+/K+-ATPase): Final common pathway - PPIs (omeprazole): Irreversibly inhibit H+/K+-ATPase - H2 blockers (ranitidine): Block histamine H2 receptors

docs.title: 1. Cephalic Phase (30%)
docs.text: Stimulus: Sight, smell, taste, thought of food Mediator: Vagus nerve (acetylcholine) Mechanism: - Direct: ACh → Parietal cells - Indirect: ACh → G cells → Gastrin

docs.title: 2. Gastric Phase (60%)
docs.text: Stimulus: Food in stomach (distension, protein) Mediators: Gastrin (main), vagus Mechanism: - Distension → Vagal reflex + Local reflexes - Proteins → G cells → Gastrin

docs.title: 3. Intestinal Phase (10%)
docs.text:
Stimulus: Food in duodenum Effect: Initially stimulatory, then inhibitory Inhibitory mediators: - Secretin (from S cells): ↓ Gastrin - CCK (from I cells): ↓ Gastric emptying - GIP (Glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide)

NEET PG: Gastric phase = Maximum secretion (60%)

docs.title: Pepsin
docs.text:
Secreted as: Pepsinogen (inactive zymogen) by chief cells

Activation: 

Pepsinogen → Pepsin (by HCl, pH <5)
Pepsinogen → Pepsin (by pepsin - autocatalysis)

Function: Protein → Peptides (breaks peptide bonds, especially aromatic amino acids)

Optimal pH: 1.5-2.5

NEET PG: Pepsin activated by HCl, optimal pH 1.5-2.5

docs.title: Intrinsic Factor
docs.text:
Secreted by: Parietal cells Function: Binds Vitamin B12 → Absorption in terminal ileum

Deficiency: - Pernicious anemia: Autoimmune destruction of parietal cells - Features: Megaloblastic anemia + neurological symptoms (subacute combined degeneration)

NEET PG: Intrinsic factor required for B12 absorption in terminal ileum

docs.title: Gastric Mucosal Barrier
docs.text:
Protection mechanisms: 1. Mucus layer: Bicarbonate-containing gel 2. Bicarbonate secretion: Neutralizes H+ 3. Tight junctions: Prevent back-diffusion of H+ 4. Blood flow: Removes excess H+ 5. Prostaglandins: ↑ Mucus, ↑ HCO3-, ↑ Blood flow

Damaged by: - NSAIDs: ↓ Prostaglandins (COX inhibition) - H. pylori: Urease → Ammonia (damages mucosa) - Alcohol, bile salts

NEET PG: NSAIDs damage gastric mucosa by inhibiting prostaglandins

docs.title: Composition
docs.text:
Daily volume: 1-2 L pH: 8-8.3 (highly alkaline - rich in HCO3-)

Two components: 1. Aqueous component (from duct cells): HCO3-, water 2. Enzymatic component (from acinar cells): Digestive enzymes

docs.title: Pancreatic Enzymes
docs.text:
Proteolytic (secreted as inactive zymogens): 1. Trypsinogen → Trypsin (by enterokinase in duodenum) 2. Chymotrypsinogen → Chymotrypsin (by trypsin) 3. Proelastase → Elastase (by trypsin) 4. Procarboxypeptidase → Carboxypeptidase (by trypsin)

Lipolytic: 1. Pancreatic lipase: Triglycerides → Fatty acids + Monoglycerides - Requires colipase and bile salts 2. Phospholipase A2: Phospholipids

Amylolytic: 1. Pancreatic amylase: Starch → Maltose, maltotriose, α-limit dextrins

Nucleases: RNase, DNase

NEET PG High-Yield: - Trypsinogen activated by enterokinase (in duodenal brush border) - Trypsin activates other pancreatic zymogens (key enzyme) - Pancreatic lipase requires colipase (overcomes bile salt inhibition)

docs.title: Aqueous Component (HCO3-)
docs.text:
Stimulated by: Secretin (from duodenal S cells) Stimulus for secretin: Acid in duodenum (pH <4.5)

Mechanism: Secretin → Pancreatic duct cells → ↑ cAMP → HCO3- secretion

docs.title: Enzymatic Component
docs.text:
Stimulated by: CCK (Cholecystokinin) (from duodenal I cells) Stimulus for CCK: Fats & proteins in duodenum

Mechanism: CCK → Pancreatic acinar cells → Enzyme secretion

Vagus nerve: Potentiates both (via ACh)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Secretin: ↑ HCO3- (neutralizes acid) - CCK: ↑ Enzymes (digests fats & proteins) - Secretin + CCK: Synergistic effect

docs.title: Composition
docs.text:
Daily secretion: 500-1000 ml pH: 7.8-8.6 (alkaline)

Components: - Bile salts (67%): Primary (cholic, chenodeoxycholic), Secondary (deoxycholic, lithocholic) - Bile pigments: Bilirubin (conjugated) - Cholesterol - Phospholipids: Lecithin - Electrolytes: Na+, K+, HCO3-

NEET PG: Bile salts synthesized in liver from cholesterol

docs.title: Functions of Bile
docs.text:
1. Fat digestion: Emulsification (↑ surface area for lipase)
  2. Fat-soluble vitamin absorption: A, D, E, K
  3. Excretion: Bilirubin, cholesterol, drugs
  4. Antimicrobial: Bile salts

NEET PG: Bile salts essential for fat-soluble vitamin absorption

docs.title: Enterohepatic Circulation
docs.text:
Bile salts: - Secreted in bile (liver) - Reabsorbed in terminal ileum (95%) - Return to liver via portal blood - Recycled 6-8 times/day

Interruption: - Ileal resection: ↓ Bile salt reabsorption → Steatorrhea, cholesterol gallstones - Cholestyramine: Binds bile salts (↓ cholesterol)

NEET PG: 95% bile salts reabsorbed in terminal ileum (enterohepatic circulation)

docs.title: Gallbladder Function
docs.text:
Storage & concentration: 5-10× concentrated Stimulated to contract by: CCK Relaxation of sphincter of Oddi: CCK, vagus

NEET PG: CCK causes gallbladder contraction + sphincter of Oddi relaxation

docs.title: GI HORMONES - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Hormone Source Stimulus Actions Gastrin G cells (antrum) Proteins, distension, vagus ↑ HCl, ↑ gastric motility, trophic (mucosa) Secretin S cells (duodenum) Acid (pH <4.5) ↑ Pancreatic HCO3-, ↑ Bile HCO3-, ↓ Gastric acid CCK I cells (duodenum) Fats, proteins ↑ Pancreatic enzymes, ↑ GB contraction, ↓ Gastric emptying GIP K cells (duodenum) Glucose, fats ↑ Insulin (incretin), ↓ Gastric acid Motilin M cells (duodenum) Fasting ↑ Migrating motor complex (MMC) VIP Neurons - ↑ Intestinal secretion, relaxes smooth muscle Somatostatin D cells Acid, fats Inhibits all GI hormones & secretions

NEET PG High-Yield: - Gastrin: ↑ HCl (most potent stimulator) - Secretin: ↑ Pancreatic HCO3- (neutralizes acid) - CCK: ↑ Pancreatic enzymes + GB contraction - GIP: Incretin (↑ insulin) - "Gastric Inhibitory Peptide" - Motilin: MMC (interdigestive motility) - Somatostatin: Universal inhibitor

docs.title: Carbohydrate Absorption
docs.text:
Digestion: 1. Salivary amylase: Starch → Maltose (mouth) 2. Pancreatic amylase: Starch → Maltose, maltotriose, α-limit dextrins (duodenum) 3. Brush border enzymes: - Maltase: Maltose → 2 Glucose - Sucrase: Sucrose → Glucose + Fructose - Lactase: Lactose → Glucose + Galactose - Isomaltase: α-limit dextrins → Glucose

Absorption (into enterocyte): - Glucose & Galactose: SGLT1 (Na+-coupled, secondary active transport) - Fructose: GLUT5 (facilitated diffusion)

Exit (into blood): - All monosaccharides: GLUT2 (basolateral, facilitated diffusion)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Glucose absorption: SGLT1 (apical), GLUT2 (basolateral) - Lactase deficiency: Lactose intolerance (osmotic diarrhea)

docs.title: Protein Absorption
docs.text:
Digestion: 1. Pepsin (stomach): Proteins → Peptides 2. Pancreatic proteases: Trypsin, chymotrypsin, elastase, carboxypeptidase 3. Brush border peptidases: Aminopeptidase, dipeptidases

Absorption: - Amino acids: Na+-coupled cotransport (multiple transporters) - Di/tripeptides: PepT1 (H+-coupled) - Intact proteins: Pinocytosis (small amount - antibodies in neonates)

NEET PG: Dipeptides absorbed via PepT1 (H+-coupled transporter)

docs.title: Fat Absorption - MOST COMPLEX
docs.text:
Digestion: 1. Lingual lipase (saliva): Starts in mouth 2. Gastric lipase: Continues in stomach 3. Pancreatic lipase (main enzyme): Triglycerides → 2-Monoglyceride + 2 Fatty acids - Requires colipase and bile salts

Emulsification: Bile salts break fat into small droplets

Micelle formation: - Bile salts + Monoglycerides + Fatty acids + Fat-soluble vitamins - Micelles transport to enterocyte

Absorption: - Short/medium-chain fatty acids (<12 C): Directly into portal blood - Long-chain fatty acids + Monoglycerides: - Re-esterified to triglycerides in enterocyte - Packaged into chylomicrons (with apolipoprotein B-48) - Released into lymphatics (lacteals) → Thoracic duct → Blood

NEET PG High-Yield: - Pancreatic lipase requires colipase & bile salts - Chylomicrons: Transport dietary fats via lymphatics - Abetalipoproteinemia: Cannot form chylomicrons (no apoB-48)

docs.title: Vitamin Absorption
docs.text:
Fat-soluble (A, D, E, K): - Require bile salts and micelles - Absorbed with fats into lymphatics

Water-soluble: - B12: Intrinsic factor → Terminal ileum (specific receptor) - Folate: Proximal jejunum - Others: Various mechanisms

NEET PG: - Fat-soluble vitamins: Need bile salts (steatorrhea → deficiency) - B12: Terminal ileum (ileal resection → deficiency)

docs.title: Mineral Absorption
docs.text:
Iron: - Absorbed as Fe2+ (ferrous) in duodenum - DMT1 (divalent metal transporter 1) - Stored as ferritin - Exported by ferroportin - Regulated by hepcidin (↓ absorption when iron stores high)

Calcium: - Active transport in duodenum & jejunum - Stimulated by Vitamin D (↑ calbindin) - Passive diffusion in ileum

NEET PG: - Iron: Absorbed in duodenum as Fe2+ - Calcium: Vitamin D-dependent (duodenum & jejunum)

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
1. Water & electrolyte absorption: 1-2 L/day
  2. Storage of feces
  3. Bacterial fermentation: Short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs)
  4. Synthesis: Vitamin K, B vitamins (by bacteria)
  5. Defecation

docs.title: Absorption
docs.text:
• Na+ absorption: Aldosterone-sensitive (ENaC channels)
  • K+ secretion: Aldosterone stimulates
  • Water follows Na+ (osmosis)

NEET PG: Aldosterone ↑ Na+ absorption & K+ secretion in colon

docs.title: Esophageal Motility
docs.text:
Primary peristalsis: Initiated by swallowing Secondary peristalsis: Initiated by esophageal distension (clears reflux)

Lower Esophageal Sphincter (LES): - Tonic contraction (prevents reflux) - Relaxes during swallowing (VIP, NO)

Achalasia: - Defect: Loss of myenteric plexus neurons - Features: ↑ LES tone, absent peristalsis - Clinical: Dysphagia to solids & liquids, regurgitation - Barium swallow: Bird's beak appearance

NEET PG: Achalasia = ↑ LES tone + absent peristalsis

docs.title: Gastric Emptying
docs.text:
Factors ↑ Emptying: - Gastric distension - Liquid consistency - Low caloric content

Factors ↓ Emptying: - Fat (most potent inhibitor - CCK) - Acid (secretin) - Hypertonicity - Amino acids

NEET PG: Fat most potent inhibitor of gastric emptying (via CCK)

docs.title: Small Intestinal Motility
docs.text:
Segmentation: Mixing (predominates during digestion) Peristalsis: Propulsion

Migrating Motor Complex (MMC): - Fasting state (between meals) - Clears undigested material - Stimulated by motilin - 3 phases: Quiescence → Irregular → Regular contractions

NEET PG: Motilin stimulates MMC (interdigestive motility)

docs.title: Colonic Motility
docs.text:
Haustral contractions: Mixing Mass movements: 1-3 times/day (after meals - gastrocolic reflex)

Defecation Reflex: - Rectal distension → Relaxation of internal anal sphincter (involuntary) - External anal sphincter: Voluntary control

docs.title: 1. Osmotic Diarrhea
docs.text:
Cause: Non-absorbed solutes (lactose, Mg2+, sorbitol) Features: - Stops with fasting - Stool osmotic gap >50 - Normal stool Na+ & K+

Stool osmotic gap = 290 - 2(stool Na+ + K+)

docs.title: 2. Secretory Diarrhea
docs.text:
Cause: ↑ Secretion or ↓ Absorption (cholera toxin, VIP) Features: - Continues despite fasting - Stool osmotic gap <50 - High volume

NEET PG: Cholera toxin → ↑ cAMP → ↑ Cl- secretion

docs.title: 3. Inflammatory Diarrhea
docs.text: Cause: IBD, infections (Shigella, Salmonella) Features: Blood, WBCs in stool, fever

docs.title: 4. Motility Disorder
docs.text:
Cause: IBS, hyperthyroidism, diabetic neuropathy

NEET PG: Osmotic gap differentiates osmotic from secretory diarrhea

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Enzyme activating trypsinogen? Answer: Enterokinase

  2. Hormone stimulating pancreatic HCO3-? Answer: Secretin

  3. Site of B12 absorption? Answer: Terminal ileum

  4. Site of bile salt reabsorption? Answer: Terminal ileum

  5. Glucose absorption transporter (apical)? Answer: SGLT1

  6. Most potent inhibitor of gastric emptying? Answer: Fat (via CCK)

  7. Phase of gastric secretion with maximum output? Answer: Gastric phase (60%)

  8. Defect in achalasia? Answer: Loss of myenteric plexus neurons

docs.title: GI Hormones (Site of Action)
docs.text: "Gut Sends Careful Chemical Messages" - **G**astrin: Gastric acid - **S**ecretin: Secretes bicarbonate - **C**CK: Contracts gallbladder - **M**otilin: MMC

docs.title: Pancreatic Enzyme Activation
docs.text: "**E**nterokinase **T**rips **T**rypsinogen, **T**rypsin **T**akes **O**ver" - **E**nterokinase activates trypsinogen - **T**rypsin activates all other zymogens

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know salivary gland contributions
  • Understand HCl secretion mechanism
  • Learn phases of gastric secretion
  • Memorize pancreatic enzymes
  • Know GI hormones (source, stimulus, action)
  • Understand carbohydrate absorption
  • Learn fat digestion & absorption
  • Know vitamin absorption sites
  • Understand diarrhea mechanisms
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on GI physiology

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (5-7 questions)

docs.title: THYROID DISORDERS - NEET PG Study Material
Anatomy - Quick Review
docs.text:
Location: Anterior neck (C5-T1) Lobes: Right & Left connected by isthmus Weight: 15-20g Blood Supply: - Superior thyroid artery (1st branch of ECA) - Inferior thyroid artery (from thyrocervical trunk) - Ima artery (10%, directly from aorta)

Venous Drainage: - Superior, middle, inferior thyroid veins

Nerves (SURGICAL IMPORTANCE): 1. Recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN): - Close relation with inferior thyroid artery - Injury → Hoarseness, bilateral injury → Stridor 2. External branch of superior laryngeal nerve: - Close to superior thyroid vessels - Injury → Cannot increase pitch

NEET PG: RLN injury most common complication of thyroid surgery

docs.title: Thyroid Function Tests
docs.text:
Test Normal Range Indication TSH 0.4-4.0 mIU/L Screening test Free T4 0.8-1.8 ng/dL Hypothyroidism Free T3 2.3-4.2 pg/mL Hyperthyroidism (T3 toxicosis) Anti-TPO Negative Autoimmune thyroid disease Thyroglobulin <50 ng/mL Tumor marker (thyroid cancer)

NEET PG: TSH is best screening test (most sensitive)

docs.title: GOITER
docs.text: Definition: Enlargement of thyroid gland

docs.title: By Function
docs.text:
• Euthyroid goiter: Normal thyroid function
  • Toxic goiter: Hyperthyroid
  • Hypothyroid goiter: Hypothyroid

docs.title: By Anatomy
docs.text:
• Diffuse: Entire gland enlarged (Graves', iodine deficiency)
  • Nodular:
  • Solitary (adenoma, cyst, cancer)
  • Multinodular (MNG)

docs.title: Endemic Goiter
docs.text: Cause: Iodine deficiency (most common worldwide) Pathophysiology: ↓ Iodine → ↓ T4 → ↑ TSH → Goiter Prevention: Iodized salt

docs.title: Sporadic Goiter
docs.text:
Causes: - Goitrogens (cabbage, cassava) - Drugs (lithium, amiodarone) - Enzyme defects

Clinical: Asymptomatic neck swelling, euthyroid

NEET PG: Iodine deficiency = Most common cause of goiter worldwide

docs.title: MULTINODULAR GOITER (MNG)
docs.text:
Definition: Multiple nodules in thyroid

Pathophysiology: Long-standing simple goiter → Nodule formation

Clinical Features: - Neck swelling (cosmetic concern) - Compressive symptoms: - Dysphagia (esophagus) - Dyspnea, stridor (trachea) - SVC obstruction - Hoarseness (RLN) - Pemberton's sign: Raise arms above head → Facial flushing, distress (SVC compression)

Retrosternal goiter: Extension into mediastinum (>50% below sternal notch)

Investigations: - Thyroid function tests: Usually euthyroid - USG neck: Multiple nodules - FNAC: Rule out malignancy - CXR/CT: Retrosternal extension, tracheal deviation

Treatment: - Conservative: If asymptomatic, small - Thyroxine suppression: Limited role - Surgery (Total thyroidectomy): - Compressive symptoms - Retrosternal extension - Suspicion of malignancy - Cosmesis

NEET PG: - Pemberton's sign: MNG with retrosternal extension - Malignancy risk: 5% in MNG

docs.title: HYPERTHYROIDISM
docs.text: Definition: ↑ Thyroid hormones

docs.title: Causes
docs.text:
• Graves' disease (70-80%) - Most common
  • Toxic multinodular goiter (Plummer's disease)
  • Toxic adenoma
  • Subacute thyroiditis (transient)
  • Exogenous thyroid hormone

docs.title: GRAVES' DISEASE
docs.text:
Pathophysiology: Autoimmune (TSH receptor antibodies → Stimulate thyroid)

Clinical Features: 1. Hyperthyroidism: - Weight loss despite ↑ appetite - Heat intolerance, sweating - Tremors, anxiety, irritability - Palpitations, tachycardia, AF - Diarrhea - Proximal muscle weakness - Menstrual irregularities

  1. Diffuse goiter: Smooth, non-tender, bruit present

  2. Ophthalmopathy (30-50%):

  3. Exophthalmos (proptosis)
  4. Lid lag, lid retraction
  5. Diplopia

  6. Corneal ulceration

  7. Pretibial myxedema (dermopathy) (5%)

  8. Thyroid acropachy (clubbing) - Rare

NEET PG High-Yield: - Triad: Hyperthyroidism + Ophthalmopathy + Pretibial myxedema - Only Graves' causes ophthalmopathy - TSI (Thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin): Pathognomonic

Investigations: - ↓ TSH, ↑ Free T4/T3 - TSI antibodies: Positive - Radioiodine uptake: Diffusely increased - USG: Diffuse enlargement, increased vascularity

Treatment:

1. Medical (1st line): - Antithyroid drugs (ATD): - Carbimazole/Methimazole: Preferred (once-daily) - Propylthiouracil (PTU): 1st trimester pregnancy, thyroid storm - Mechanism: Inhibit thyroid peroxidase (↓ T4/T3 synthesis) - Duration: 12-18 months - Adverse effects: - Agranulocytosis (check if fever, sore throat) - Hepatotoxicity (PTU > carbimazole) - Rash, arthralgia

2. Beta-blockers (Propranolol): - Symptomatic relief - Blocks peripheral conversion T4 → T3

3. Radioiodine (I-131): - Indications: Relapse after ATD, elderly, poor surgical candidate - Mechanism: Destroys thyroid tissue - Contraindications: Pregnancy, lactation, severe ophthalmopathy - Complication: Hypothyroidism (60-80%)

4. Surgery (Total thyroidectomy): - Indications: - Large goiter (compressive symptoms) - Pregnancy (2nd trimester) - Suspicion of malignancy - Patient preference - Preparation: - ATD until euthyroid - Lugol's iodine (10-14 days before surgery) - ↓ Vascularity - Beta-blocker - Complications: RLN injury, hypoparathyroidism, hypothyroidism, thyroid storm

NEET PG: - PTU: Preferred in pregnancy (1st trimester) & thyroid storm (blocks T4→T3 conversion) - Lugol's iodine: Pre-op to ↓ vascularity (10-14 days) - I-131: Contraindicated in pregnancy

docs.title: THYROID STORM (Thyrotoxic Crisis)
docs.text:
Definition: Life-threatening hyperthyroidism

Triggers: - Surgery (inadequate preparation) - Infection, trauma, DKA - Withdrawal of ATD - Radioiodine therapy

Clinical Features (Burch-Wartofsky criteria): - High fever (>40°C) - Tachycardia (>140 bpm), AF - CNS: Agitation, confusion, seizures, coma - GI: Diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice - Cardiovascular collapse

NEET PG: Diagnosis clinical (no time for labs)

Treatment (ABCDE): 1. Antithyroid drugs: PTU 600mg loading → 200mg q6h (preferred - blocks conversion) 2. Beta-blocker: Propranolol 40-80mg q6h (or IV if severe) 3. Corticosteroids: Hydrocortisone 100mg q8h (blocks T4→T3, treats adrenal insufficiency) 4. Dexamethasone: Alternative to hydrocortisone 5. Iodine: Lugol's iodine 5 drops q6h (give 1 hour after ATD)

Additional: - Cooling blankets (avoid aspirin - ↑ free T4) - IV fluids - Treat precipitating cause

NEET PG High-Yield: - Give iodine 1 hour AFTER ATD (prevents iodine use for hormone synthesis) - PTU preferred over carbimazole (blocks peripheral conversion) - Avoid aspirin (displaces T4 from proteins)

Mortality: 20-30% (medical emergency)

docs.title: Causes
docs.text:
Primary (95%): - Hashimoto's thyroiditis (most common in iodine-sufficient areas) - Iodine deficiency - Post-thyroidectomy - Post-radioiodine - Drugs (amiodarone, lithium)

Secondary: Pituitary failure

Tertiary: Hypothalamic failure

NEET PG: Hashimoto's = Most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient areas

docs.title: HASHIMOTO'S THYROIDITIS
docs.text:
Pathophysiology: Autoimmune (anti-TPO, anti-thyroglobulin antibodies)

Clinical: - Hypothyroidism: Weight gain, cold intolerance, fatigue, constipation, bradycardia, dry skin - Goiter: Firm, non-tender, diffuse

Histology: - Lymphocytic infiltration - Hurthle cells (oxyphilic cells) - Germinal centers

Associations: - Other autoimmune diseases (Type 1 DM, vitiligo, Addison's) - Slightly ↑ risk of thyroid lymphoma

NEET PG: - Anti-TPO antibodies: Hashimoto's - Hurthle cells: Hashimoto's (also in Graves')

Treatment: Levothyroxine (lifelong)

docs.title: THYROID NODULES - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Prevalence: 4-7% by palpation, 50-60% on USG

Malignancy risk: 5-15%

docs.title: Evaluation
docs.text:
History: - High-risk features: - Age <20 or >60 years - Male gender - Rapid growth - Hoarseness, dysphagia - Family history of thyroid cancer - Radiation exposure (childhood)

Clinical: - Suspicious features: - Hard, fixed nodule - Cervical lymphadenopathy - Vocal cord palsy

Investigations:

1. TSH: - Low TSH: Hyperfunctioning (likely benign) → Thyroid scan - Normal/High TSH: → USG + FNAC

2. Thyroid USG: - High-risk features: Hypoechoic, microcalcifications, irregular margins, taller than wide, ↑ vascularity

3. FNAC (Gold standard): - Bethesda classification: - I: Non-diagnostic → Repeat - II: Benign → Follow-up - III/IV: Indeterminate → Molecular testing or surgery - V: Suspicious → Surgery - VI: Malignant → Surgery

NEET PG: - FNAC: Most important investigation - Bethesda II (Benign) → Observe - Bethesda VI (Malignant) → Surgery

docs.title: Classification
docs.text: Type Percentage Prognosis Features Papillary 80% Excellent Psammoma bodies, Orphan Annie nuclei Follicular 10-15% Good Hematogenous spread (bone, lung) Medullary 5% Intermediate Calcitonin, amyloid, MEN 2A/2B Anaplastic 1-2% Poor (months) Elderly, rapidly fatal

docs.title: PAPILLARY THYROID CANCER (PTC)
docs.text:
Most common thyroid cancer (80%)

Risk Factors: - Radiation exposure (childhood) - Strongest risk factor - Young age - Female

Pathology: - Psammoma bodies (laminated calcifications) - Orphan Annie eye nuclei (ground-glass, empty nuclei) - Nuclear grooves - Intranuclear inclusions

Spread: Lymphatic (cervical lymph nodes) >> Hematogenous

Subtypes: - Tall cell variant: Aggressive - Follicular variant: Good prognosis

NEET PG High-Yield: - Most common thyroid cancer - Psammoma bodies = Papillary carcinoma - Lymphatic spread (cervical LN) - Best prognosis

Treatment: - Total thyroidectomy + Central lymph node dissection (if LN+) - Radioiodine ablation (I-131) post-op - TSH suppression (levothyroxine to keep TSH low)

Follow-up: Thyroglobulin (tumor marker)

docs.title: FOLLICULAR THYROID CANCER (FTC)
docs.text:
Second most common (10-15%)

Pathology: - Cannot diagnose on FNAC (requires capsular/vascular invasion) - Histology: Capsular or vascular invasion

Spread: Hematogenous (bone, lung) >> Lymphatic

NEET PG: - Cannot differentiate from follicular adenoma on FNAC (needs histology) - Hematogenous spread (bone, lung)

Treatment: Same as PTC (total thyroidectomy + radioiodine)

docs.title: MEDULLARY THYROID CANCER (MTC)
docs.text:
Arises from: Parafollicular C cells (produce calcitonin)

Types: - Sporadic (75%) - Familial (25%): - MEN 2A: MTC + Pheochromocytoma + Hyperparathyroidism - MEN 2B: MTC + Pheochromocytoma + Mucosal neuromas + Marfanoid habitus - Familial MTC (FMTC)

Pathology: - Amyloid stroma (from calcitonin) - RET proto-oncogene mutation

Tumor Markers: - Calcitonin: Diagnostic & follow-up (↑ with pentagastrin/Ca2+ stimulation) - CEA: Prognosis

NEET PG High-Yield: - MEN 2A & 2B: Associated with MTC - Calcitonin: Tumor marker - Amyloid deposition: MTC - Screen for pheochromocytoma before surgery (MEN 2)

Treatment: - Total thyroidectomy + Central LN dissection - NO role for radioiodine (C cells don't take up iodine)

Prophylactic thyroidectomy: Children with RET mutation (MEN 2)

docs.title: ANAPLASTIC THYROID CANCER
docs.text:
Most aggressive thyroid cancer

Age: Elderly (>60 years)

Clinical: Rapidly growing neck mass, dysphagia, hoarseness, stridor

Pathology: Undifferentiated, pleomorphic cells

Prognosis: Median survival 6 months

NEET PG: Rapidly fatal, no role for surgery (unless for airway)

Treatment: Palliative (radiation, chemotherapy, tracheostomy if airway obstruction)

docs.title: Immediate
docs.text:
1. Hemorrhage: Most dangerous (can cause airway obstruction)

  2. Management: Open wound at bedside, re-explore

  3. Airway obstruction: Hematoma, bilateral RLN injury, tracheomalacia

  4. Thyroid storm: Inadequate preparation

docs.title: Early (1 week)
docs.text:
1. Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (1-2%):
  2. Unilateral: Hoarseness, weak voice

  3. Bilateral: Stridor, respiratory distress (emergency tracheostomy)

  4. Superior laryngeal nerve (external branch) injury:

  5. Cannot increase pitch (singers)

  6. Hypoparathyroidism (transient or permanent):

  7. Hypocalcemia: Perioral numbness, tetany, Chvostek's sign, Trousseau's sign
  8. Treatment: IV calcium gluconate (emergency), oral calcium + vitamin D

docs.title: Late
docs.text:
1. Hypothyroidism: All patients (on levothyroxine)
  2. Keloid/scar: Cosmetic

NEET PG High-Yield: - RLN injury: Most common nerve injury - Bilateral RLN injury: Stridor (emergency tracheostomy) - Hypoparathyroidism: Check Ca2+ post-op - Hemorrhage: Open wound at bedside if airway compromise

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Most common thyroid cancer? Answer: Papillary carcinoma

  2. Psammoma bodies seen in? Answer: Papillary thyroid carcinoma

  3. Tumor marker for medullary thyroid cancer? Answer: Calcitonin

  4. Most common complication of thyroid surgery? Answer: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury

  5. Drug of choice for thyroid storm? Answer: PTU (blocks synthesis + peripheral conversion)

  6. Pemberton's sign indicates? Answer: Retrosternal goiter with SVC compression

  7. Most common cause of hyperthyroidism? Answer: Graves' disease

  8. Hashimoto's thyroiditis antibody? Answer: Anti-TPO

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know thyroid anatomy (RLN, superior laryngeal nerve)
  • Understand Graves' disease (clinical, treatment)
  • Learn thyroid storm management
  • Know thyroid cancer types (pathology, prognosis)
  • Memorize complications of thyroid surgery
  • Understand FNAC Bethesda classification
  • Learn MEN 2 syndromes
  • Practice 35+ MCQs on thyroid

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 4-6 questions)

docs.title: ACUTE ABDOMEN - NEET PG Study Material
Definition
docs.text:
Acute abdomen: Sudden onset severe abdominal pain requiring urgent evaluation and often surgical intervention

Key principle: "Never let the sun set or rise on an acute abdomen without a diagnosis"

docs.title: Right Upper Quadrant (RUQ)
docs.text:
• Acute cholecystitis
  • Acute cholangitis
  • Biliary colic
  • Hepatic abscess
  • Perforated duodenal ulcer
  • Acute hepatitis
  • Right lower lobe pneumonia
  • Budd-Chiari syndrome

docs.title: Right Lower Quadrant (RLQ)
docs.text:
• Acute appendicitis (most common)
  • Meckel's diverticulitis
  • Ileitis (Crohn's)
  • Ruptured ovarian cyst
  • Ectopic pregnancy
  • Ovarian torsion
  • PID (Pelvic Inflammatory Disease)
  • Psoas abscess

docs.title: Left Upper Quadrant (LUQ)
docs.text:
• Splenic rupture
  • Splenic infarction
  • Gastric ulcer perforation
  • Acute pancreatitis

docs.title: Left Lower Quadrant (LLQ)
docs.text:
• Diverticulitis
  • Sigmoid volvulus
  • Ruptured ovarian cyst
  • Ectopic pregnancy
  • Ovarian torsion
  • PID

docs.title: Periumbilical/Generalized
docs.text:
• Small bowel obstruction
  • Perforated peptic ulcer
  • Acute pancreatitis
  • Mesenteric ischemia
  • Ruptured AAA
  • Peritonitis

NEET PG: Acute appendicitis = Most common surgical cause of acute abdomen

docs.title: History (SOCRATES)
docs.text: **S**ite: Location **O**nset: Sudden vs gradual **C**haracter: Colicky, sharp, dull, burning **R**adiation: Referred pain **A**ssociations: Nausea, vomiting, fever **T**ime course: Duration, progression **E**xacerbating/relieving factors **S**everity: Pain scale

docs.title: Important Questions
docs.text:
• Last menstrual period (females - r/o pregnancy)
  • Past medical/surgical history
  • Medications (anticoagulants, steroids)
  • Trauma

docs.title: Inspection
docs.text:
• Position: Lying still (peritonitis) vs writhing (colic)
  • Distension: Obstruction, ascites
  • Visible peristalsis: Obstruction
  • Scars: Previous surgery (adhesions)

docs.title: Palpation
docs.text:
• Tenderness: Localized vs generalized
  • Guarding: Voluntary (pain) vs Involuntary (peritonitis)
  • Rigidity: Board-like abdomen (perforation)
  • Rebound tenderness (Blumberg's sign): Peritonitis
  • Mass: Tumor, abscess, AAA

docs.title: Percussion
docs.text:
• Tympany: Gas (obstruction, ileus)
  • Dullness: Fluid (ascites), mass
  • Shifting dullness: Ascites
  • Obliteration of liver dullness: Pneumoperitoneum (perforation)

docs.title: Auscultation
docs.text:
• High-pitched tinkling sounds: Obstruction
  • Absent bowel sounds: Ileus, peritonitis
  • Bruit: AAA

NEET PG: Absent bowel sounds + rigidity = Peritonitis

docs.title: Special Signs - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Sign Technique Positive in Murphy's sign RUQ palpation during deep inspiration → Arrest Acute cholecystitis Rovsing's sign LLQ palpation → RLQ pain Acute appendicitis McBurney's point Tenderness ⅓ distance from ASIS to umbilicus Acute appendicitis Psoas sign Pain on hip extension (right) Retrocecal appendicitis, psoas abscess Obturator sign Pain on internal rotation of flexed hip Pelvic appendicitis Cullen's sign Periumbilical ecchymosis Acute pancreatitis, ruptured ectopic Grey Turner's sign Flank ecchymosis Acute pancreatitis, AAA rupture Courvoisier's sign Palpable, non-tender gallbladder + jaundice Pancreatic head cancer (NOT stones) Kehr's sign Left shoulder pain (referred) Splenic rupture Carnett's sign ↑ Pain when lifting head (tensing abdominal muscles) Abdominal wall pain (NOT visceral)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Murphy's sign: Cholecystitis - Rovsing's sign: Appendicitis - Cullen's + Grey Turner's: Hemorrhagic pancreatitis - Courvoisier's law: Palpable GB + jaundice = Malignancy (NOT stones)

docs.title: Laboratory
docs.text:
Mandatory: - CBC: Leukocytosis (infection), anemia (bleeding) - Serum amylase/lipase: Pancreatitis (lipase more specific) - LFTs: Cholangitis, hepatitis - Serum electrolytes: Dehydration - Urine pregnancy test: All women of childbearing age - Urinalysis: UTI, stones

Others: - Serum lactate: ↑ in mesenteric ischemia - Coagulation profile: Before surgery - Blood group & cross-match: If bleeding suspected - ABG: Metabolic acidosis (ischemia, sepsis)

NEET PG: β-hCG (pregnancy test) mandatory in all females with acute abdomen

docs.title: X-ray Abdomen (Erect + Supine)
docs.text:
Erect CXR: - Free gas under diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum): Perforation - Rigler's sign: Both sides of bowel wall visible - Football sign: Large pneumoperitoneum (oval lucency)

Erect AXR: - Air-fluid levels: Obstruction - Dilated bowel loops: Obstruction - Sentinel loop: Localized ileus (pancreatitis, appendicitis) - Psoas shadow loss: Retroperitoneal pathology - Calcifications: Gallstones, renal stones, aortic calcification

Supine AXR: - Dilated bowel: Obstruction - Thumbprinting: Ischemic colitis, IBD - Gas in bowel wall (pneumatosis): Ischemia - Portal venous gas: Mesenteric ischemia (grave sign)

NEET PG: - Pneumoperitoneum: Best seen on erect CXR (free gas under diaphragm) - Portal venous gas: Mesenteric ischemia (poor prognosis)

docs.title: Ultrasound (USG)
docs.text:
First-line for: - RUQ pain: Gallstones, cholecystitis, cholangitis - Pelvic pain (females): Ectopic pregnancy, ovarian torsion, PID - AAA: Screening, rupture

Advantages: Bedside, no radiation, cheap

NEET PG: USG first-line for biliary pathology & gynecological causes

docs.title: CT Scan (CECT Abdomen)
docs.text:
Gold standard for acute abdomen

Indications: - Unclear diagnosis - Trauma - Suspected perforation (if X-ray negative) - AAA rupture - Mesenteric ischemia - Appendicitis (if diagnosis unclear) - Diverticulitis - Pancreatitis (complications)

Findings: - Free air: Perforation - Stranding: Inflammation (appendicitis, diverticulitis, pancreatitis) - Abscess - Vascular abnormalities

NEET PG: CT = Gold standard for acute abdomen evaluation

docs.title: ACUTE APPENDICITIS - HIGH YIELD
docs.text: Most common surgical emergency

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
1. Luminal obstruction (fecalith, lymphoid hyperplasia, worms)
  2. Bacterial overgrowth
  3. Inflammation
  4. Ischemia & necrosis
  5. Perforation (if untreated)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Classic presentation: 1. Periumbilical pain (visceral - referred T10) 2. Anorexia, nausea, vomiting 3. Pain migrates to RLQ (McBurney's point) - somatic (parietal peritoneum) 4. Fever (low-grade initially)

Timeline: Symptoms usually <48 hours

Examination: - Tenderness at McBurney's point (⅓ from ASIS to umbilicus) - Rovsing's sign (+) - Rebound tenderness - Guarding - Psoas sign (+) if retrocecal - Obturator sign (+) if pelvic

NEET PG: Pain migration (periumbilical → RLQ) = Classic for appendicitis

docs.title: Alvarado Score (MANTRELS)
docs.text:
Feature Score **M**igration of pain to RLQ 1 **A**norexia 1 **N**ausea/vomiting 1 **T**enderness in RLQ 2 **R**ebound tenderness 1 **E**levated temperature (>37.3°C) 1 **L**eukocytosis (>10,000) 2 **S**hift to left (neutrophils >75%) 1

Total: 10 points

Interpretation: - 1-4: Unlikely appendicitis - 5-6: Possible (observe or image) - 7-8: Probable (admit, consider surgery) - 9-10: Very likely (surgery)

NEET PG: Alvarado score ≥7 → High probability of appendicitis

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
Laboratory: - Leukocytosis (10,000-18,000) with left shift - Urine pregnancy test (females) - Urinalysis (r/o UTI)

Imaging: - USG: Thickened, non-compressible appendix (>6 mm), target sign, free fluid - CT scan (if diagnosis unclear): Appendiceal thickening, periappendiceal stranding, appendicolith

NEET PG: - Appendix diameter >6 mm = Abnormal - CT: Most accurate (95% sensitivity)

docs.title: Treatment
docs.text:
Medical (initially): - NPO (nil per oral) - IV fluids - Analgesia - Antibiotics (if perforation suspected)

Surgical: Appendectomy (open or laparoscopic) - Timing: Urgent (within 24 hours) - Laparoscopic: Preferred (less pain, faster recovery)

Interval appendectomy: If appendiceal mass/abscess - Conservative management (antibiotics) - Appendectomy after 6-8 weeks

NEET PG: Laparoscopic appendectomy preferred (diagnostic + therapeutic)

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
• Perforation (20-30%): ↑ If delayed >24-48 hours
  • Appendiceal abscess (periappendiceal)
  • Peritonitis (generalized)
  • Pylephlebitis: Septic thrombophlebitis of portal vein (rare, serious)

NEET PG: Perforation risk ↑ if delay >24-48 hours

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
90% due to gallstones (calculous cholecystitis)

Sequence: 1. Stone impaction in cystic duct 2. Obstruction → Bile stasis 3. Chemical inflammation (bile salts) 4. Bacterial infection (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus) 5. Gangrene/perforation (if untreated)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Biliary colic → Acute cholecystitis: - Severe RUQ pain (constant, >6 hours) - vs colic (<6 hours) - Fever (>38°C) - Nausea, vomiting - Positive Murphy's sign - Palpable GB (if distended) - Mild jaundice (10-20%)

NEET PG: Murphy's sign = Inspiratory arrest on RUQ palpation

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
Laboratory: - Leukocytosis (12,000-15,000) - Mild ↑ bilirubin (<4 mg/dl) - Mild ↑ ALP, GGT - Amylase/lipase (r/o pancreatitis)

Imaging: - USG (first-line): - Gallstones - GB wall thickening (>3 mm) - Pericholecystic fluid - Sonographic Murphy's sign - HIDA scan (if USG equivocal): Non-filling of GB (cystic duct obstruction)

NEET PG: USG first-line for cholecystitis (Murphy's sign + GB wall thickening)

docs.title: Tokyo Guidelines (Severity)
docs.text: Grade I (Mild): No organ dysfunction Grade II (Moderate): Leukocytosis >18,000, palpable mass, duration >72 hours Grade III (Severe): Organ dysfunction (hypotension, renal/hepatic/CNS dysfunction)

docs.title: Treatment
docs.text:
Medical (initially): - NPO, IV fluids - Analgesia (NSAIDs, opioids) - Antibiotics: Broad-spectrum (ceftriaxone + metronidazole)
